title: Matelea pedunculata
taxonomy: Matelea pedunculata is a species of flowering plant in the dogbane family, Apocynaceae. It is native to South America, specifically in the tropical regions. Matelea pedunculata is classified as a member of the genus Matelea, which is a group of flowering plants that are commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions. The plant is further classified into the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Matelea pedunculata is also classified as a part of the order Gentianales, which includes other flowering plants such as gentians and dogbanes.
uses: Matelea pedunculata is known to have various medicinal uses, particularly in traditional medicine. The plant's extracts have been studied for their potential antiparasitic, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory properties. Traditional healers use Matelea pedunculata to treat various health conditions, including fever, wounds, and skin infections. Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of bioactive compounds, which have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and analgesic activities. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential benefits and applications of Matelea pedunculata in modern medicine.

Its active constituents include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. parts: Matelea pedunculata is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. Unfortunately, there is limited information available on the specific medicinal parts of Matelea pedunculata. preparations: Matelea pedunculata, also known as the 'cascabela vine', is a plant with potential medicinal properties. Herbal preparations of Matelea pedunculata have been traditionally used in various cultures, particularly in the Amazon region, to treat a range of ailments. side_effects: Matelea pedunculata, also known as the "devil's claw vine," can cause side effects due to its potential interactions with certain medications and its high levels of saponins. The consumption of Matelea pedunculata may lead to gastrointestinal problems, such as nausea, diarrhea, and stomach pain, in some individuals. Additionally, the plant may cause allergic reactions or skin irritation in people who are sensitive to its compounds. The side effects of Matelea pedunculata may also be exacerbated by its high levels of toxicity, which can be particularly problematic for young children or pets. More research is needed to fully understand the potential risks and side effects associated with the use of Matelea pedunculata.
intro: Matelea pedunculata, also known as Devil's Claw, is a perennial vine native to Central and South America, belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including reducing inflammation and pain, as well as its ability to alleviate symptoms of arthritis and other rheumatic conditions. The bioactive constituents of Matelea pedunculata include iridoid glycosides, particularly harpagoside, which has been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. Traditionally, Matelea pedunculata is used in herbal preparations such as teas, capsules, and extracts, often in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects.
